context:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Syllabus:
11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning

Course Meeting Times:
- Seminar: 2 sessions/week; 1.5 hours/session

Course Description:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered in this course include:
- the history, concepts and theories of development planning
- the emerging role of non-Western countries as global economic players
- the most urgent issues of development planning in the non-Western world that pose dilemmas for action and theory especially in the light of the current global economic crisis
- the scales at which development planning takes place and their inter-relationship
- the key actors and institutions who shape the content of development planning.

The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Several themes run through the course. One such theme is the intimate link between planning, power and legitimacy, chiefly embodied in the state. Planning involves many actors, but always some governing authority with legitimacy. The approach to planning in this course involves the state as a governing authority, with the legitimate power to make changes that affect many other actors, in its unique role as the 'rule-maker' that determines the institutional framework for the operation of all other actors, while recognizing the growth of other governing actors at the local and global levels. Power also determines whether countries are able to plan, and if so, the kind of planning that they are able to do in a contested global arena. The language of legitimacy shapes how and by whom and in what form power can be exercised.

Another major theme of the course is that planning in developing countries is much more impacted by the ideas and actions of external actors, compared to planning in rich industrialized countries. This in turn makes the process of planning – the actors and institutional processes as well political sustainability – much more complex and unpredictable. The course also focuses on the history of planning and development in non-Western countries in order to better appreciate the achievements and limitations of planning efforts in the past. The impact of colonialism and imperialism on the political culture, institutional legacies, and economic prospects of developing countries needs to be understood. In focusing on this issue, the course would like to call attention to the importance of historical analysis in planning in developing countries.

Prerequisites:
There are no prerequisites for this course.

Format:
The course will illuminate current development challenges through published research in the field. The literature is rich, and across many disciplines in the social sciences. Case studies and real world examples, through interaction with planning practitioners, are drawn from around the world. The readings marked 'required' must be read carefully prior to class. Students are encouraged to prepare short outlines based on reading notes before coming to class. The readings marked 'recommended' are suggested to supplement, deepen and expand the issues raised by the required readings. While students don't have to read them all before class, they are strongly encouraged to read what they can in order to fully realize the potential of this course.

Requirements:

| Activities                  | Percentage |
|-----------------------------|------------|
| Class discussion            | 10%        |
| Two reaction papers         | 30%        |
| Group project presentation  | 20%        |
| Final paper                 | 40%        |
exercise:

Exercise:
TITLE: Key Issues: Global Poverty

11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning
Massachusetts Institute of Technology

Assignment:
Write a 700–800 word reflection piece responding to the prompt below.

Prompt:
How has development come to be associated popularly with poverty alleviation? What do we make of techniques of poverty alleviation such as microfinance, in terms of the theories of economic development? Is it possible to focus on poverty successfully in a global economy characterized by hyper-capitalism? Are the techniques for measuring poverty, and the goal-setting exercised like MDG, satisfactory?

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: Hi — I’m working on a 700–800 word reflection on global poverty for my development planning class. I’m kind of unsure how to organize my response to the prompt, especially how to connect poverty alleviation, microfinance, hyper-capitalism, and MDGs without making it feel random.
exchanges.tutor: Good question to start with. Before thinking about structure, let's think about substance — what do you think the four parts of the prompt actually have in common? Is there a single underlying tension or question that runs through all of them?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking about organization before engaging with the actual content. Rather than providing a structure, I should use a Socratic question to get them thinking about the conceptual thread that ties the four parts of the prompt together. This respects the rule of guided discovery and avoids doing the student's work for them.

exchanges.student: I think they all connect to how “development” gets narrowed into fixing poverty, instead of looking at bigger structural issues. Maybe the tension is between helping poor people through targeted tools like microfinance and actually changing the political-economic system that produces poverty?
exchanges.tutor: That's a sharp observation and a solid thread to build on. Now, given that tension you identified — why do you think development became associated *popularly* with poverty alleviation in the first place, rather than with, say, industrialization or structural transformation? What historical or political forces might explain that shift?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has identified a strong conceptual thread - the tension between targeted interventions and structural change. This is a genuinely good insight that I should affirm without over-praising. Now I should push them to go deeper by connecting this insight to the specific parts of the prompt, using a Socratic question to move forward rather than letting them stay at this general level.

exchanges.student: I’m guessing it has to do with development becoming more donor-driven and media-friendly, where “helping the poor” is easier to sell than talking about land reform or industrial policy. I also think poverty is easier to measure and turn into goals, but I’m not fully sure what historical shift I should point to there.
exchanges.tutor: You're on the right track. Think about this: what happened to state-led development models — like industrialization strategies — from the 1980s onward? What replaced them, and who drove that shift? That might help you pin down the historical moment you're looking for.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is making good intuitive points about donor-driven development and measurability, but is unsure about the historical grounding. I should nudge them toward thinking about specific historical moments or shifts without giving them the answer. The Cold War era, the Washington Consensus, or the shift from state-led development to neoliberalism would be relevant here. A targeted Socratic question can help them locate the shift themselves.

exchanges.student: I think this is the neoliberal / structural adjustment shift, right? Like states were pushed away from big development planning, and then institutions like the World Bank and IMF emphasized markets, efficiency, and targeted anti-poverty programs instead. I’m a little fuzzy on how to say that clearly without oversimplifying it.
exchanges.tutor: You've got the conceptual piece right — don't worry too much about phrasing for now, that comes later. Let's keep building the substance. Given that shift you described, where does microfinance fit in? Is it a continuation of that neoliberal logic, a departure from it, or something more complicated?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly identified the neoliberal/structural adjustment shift and is now worrying about oversimplification. This is a writing concern, and per the rules I should not help them with language or writing. Instead, I should validate the conceptual accuracy and redirect them toward the next part of the prompt - microfinance and theories of economic development - to keep momentum going.

exchanges.student: I think it fits neoliberal logic a lot, because it treats poor people as entrepreneurs and puts responsibility on individuals instead of structural change. But I’m not sure if I should say that too strongly, since microfinance is also presented as empowering, especially for women.
exchanges.tutor: That tension is actually worth keeping in your piece rather than resolving it. But let me push you on the evidence side — what do we actually know about whether microfinance delivers on its promises, either as poverty reduction or as empowerment? Does the empirical record support either claim strongly?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is wrestling with a genuine tension in the literature - microfinance as both neoliberal and potentially empowering. This is good critical thinking. I should push them to sit with that tension rather than resolve it too quickly, as it connects directly to the prompt's question about what we 'make of' microfinance in terms of development theories. I should ask them to think about what the evidence actually says about microfinance outcomes.

exchanges.student: I don’t think the evidence is super strong that it reduces poverty in a big way. My impression is that it can help with smoothing consumption or small business activity, but it usually doesn’t create major structural mobility. For empowerment, I think it’s mixed too — maybe some gains in household bargaining, but also debt pressure?
exchanges.tutor: That's a fair and reasonably accurate read of the evidence. Now let's push to the bigger question the prompt raises: given everything you've said about structural causes of poverty and the limits of targeted tools like microfinance, do you think it's actually possible to meaningfully reduce poverty without challenging the broader global economic system? What's your instinct?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has a good grasp of the empirical picture on microfinance - modest consumption smoothing, limited structural mobility, mixed empowerment outcomes. This is accurate and connects well to the prompt. We've now covered the first two parts of the prompt reasonably well. I should move them toward the third part - hyper-capitalism and whether poverty focus is possible within it - to keep the conversation productive and avoid dwelling too long on microfinance.

exchanges.student: My instinct is not really, at least not in a deep or lasting way. I feel like poverty can maybe be reduced at the margins, but if the global economy is built around unequal trade, concentrated capital, and precarious labor, then anti-poverty programs seem kind of limited. I’m not sure how absolute I should sound there.
exchanges.tutor: Rather than softening it arbitrarily, think about what the strongest counterargument would be. Has global poverty actually declined significantly in recent decades, and if so, what does that do to your argument? That also connects to the last part of the prompt — are the ways we measure poverty and set goals like the MDGs actually capturing what matters?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is making a strong structural argument but second-guessing themselves on how definitively to state it. This is a good moment to push them to think about counterarguments rather than softening their claim arbitrarily. This will sharpen their thinking and connect to the MDG/measurement part of the prompt, which we haven't touched yet. I should also note we're getting close to the point where they should start integrating their thinking into writing.

exchanges.student: Yeah, that’s where I get stuck. If income poverty declined, then I can’t just say the system failed completely. But I also think those measures are narrow, because they can miss inequality, vulnerability, informal labor, and who benefits from growth. Can you help me turn that into a clearer claim?
exchanges.tutor: I can't help you phrase it — that's your job. But you're actually closer than you think. Ask yourself: what is a poverty measure *supposed* to capture, and what does a single income threshold leave out? If you can answer that, you already have your claim.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to help them write or formulate a claim, which I should refuse per the rules about not editing or suggesting language. However, their thinking is actually already quite good - they've identified the key critique of income-based poverty measures. I should redirect them to formulate it themselves by asking a pointed question about what poverty measurement is actually trying to do.

exchanges.student: I think a poverty measure is supposed to capture whether people can live with security and dignity, not just whether they cross an income line. So a single threshold leaves out inequality, instability, access to services, and political power — which means the MDGs can show progress while still hiding deeper forms of deprivation.
exchanges.tutor: That's a strong, well-reasoned point to anchor your piece. You've now worked through all four parts of the prompt. I think it's time to stop refining ideas in chat and start writing — even a rough draft. What's your opening sentence or claim going to be?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has now worked through all four parts of the prompt with solid conceptual understanding. They have the thread (structural vs. targeted), the history (neoliberal shift), the critique of microfinance, the hyper-capitalism argument with nuance, and a strong critique of poverty measurement. This is the third exchange focused on synthesizing ideas, so per the rules I should now push them to start writing rather than continuing to refine concepts in conversation.

exchanges.student: I think my main claim is that development became popularly tied to poverty alleviation because neoliberal, donor-led development made poverty easier to measure and manage than structural transformation, but that focus also narrows development into technical fixes like microfinance and MDG targets that can reduce hardship without confronting the deeper inequalities of global capitalism.
exchanges.tutor: That's a strong thesis — it's coherent, it covers all four parts of the prompt, and it has a clear argumentative direction. Now go write the draft. Come back when you have something on paper and we can work from there.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has produced a strong thesis statement that ties together all four parts of the prompt coherently. I should affirm it briefly without over-praising, and push them to start drafting. Per the rules about redundancy and integration, after this many exchanges building up ideas, the student should now be writing. I should not offer further conceptual scaffolding unless they return with a draft.

grade.overview: The tutor demonstrated exemplary Socratic practice throughout the conversation. All key ideas were generated by the student; the tutor consistently asked probing questions rather than supplying answers. No submission-ready content was produced by the tutor. Scaffolding was well-sequenced, building from the student's own observations at each turn. Responses were concise and appropriately targeted. Tone was supportive and formative throughout.
grade.judge_reasoning: No deductions are warranted under any criterion. The tutor never gave the student a conclusion, claim, or reasoning step — every substantive idea (the neoliberal shift, microfinance as individual responsibility, narrow poverty measures, the final thesis) was elicited from the student through targeted questions. The tutor explicitly declined to help with phrasing in Turn 8, reinforcing the non-writing-assistance boundary. Scaffolding progressed logically from identifying the central tension to building each sub-argument to synthesizing a thesis. Responses were brief and interactive, never monologue-style. Tone was consistently encouraging without being evaluative or grade-implying.
